coredata.dc:title: Ultra-stable room temperature phosphorescence CDs-based composite materials for anti-counterfeiting application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Zhao, Lu
Hao, Chenxia
Yuan, Lin
Liu, Lizhen
Yang, Xiaoming
Bai, Yunfeng
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Counterfeit and shoddy products exist widely in our daily life. Effective anti-counterfeiting means have become an issue with increasing concern. Herein, we proposed a new strategy for dispersing N, S-CDs into melamine phosphate (MP) to prepare N, S-CDs@MP composite. The co-doping of carbon dots with heteroatoms (N and S atoms) in conjunction with matrix protection synergistically enhanced the emission of long-lived room temperature phosphorescence (RTP). The obtained N, S-CDs@MP composite exhibited green RTP with phosphorescence quantum yield of 9.27 % and lifetime of 489 ms. More importantly, even in extreme conditions, the RTP of N, S-CDs@MP in the dispersion still persisted. The excellent RTP properties and good stability made N, S-CDs@MP a promising material for advanced anti-counterfeiting applications. This study afforded a new RTP material which could be easily prepared and used for information encryption and graphic anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heteroatom doping
Matrix protection
Melamine phosphate
Room temperature phosphorescence
Ultra-stable
Anti-counterfeiting

Optical properties and RTP mechanism of N, S-CDs@MP
Superb phosphorescence stability of N, S-CDs@MP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22476118 ), Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi ( 2022L424 , 2023L271 ), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province ( 202303021211324 , 202303021222212 ), Fund Program for the Scientific Activities of Selected Returned Overseas Professionals in Shanxi Province ( 20230036 ), Fundamental Research Program of Datong City ( 2023065 ), Key Scientific and Technological Projects of Datong City ( 2024054 ), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Datong University ( 2022K18 ) and Postgraduate science and technology innovation project of Shanxi Datong University ( 22CX24 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CDs, inset: N, S-CDs with daylight (A) and UV (B); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs under various excitation wavelengths; (c) Absorption spectrum of N, S-CDs; (d) TEM image of N, S-CDs and (e) their size distribution; (f) HRTEM image of N, S-CDs with lattice fringes; (g) XRD pattern of N, S-CDs; (h) FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CDs; (i) Full XPS survey of N, S-CDs.
(a) TEM image of N, S-CDs@MP and (b) their size distribution; (c) XRD patterns of N, S-CDs@MP and MP; (d) FT-IR spectra of MP, N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs@MP; (e) Full XPS survey of N, S-CDs@MP.

(a) Images of N, S-CDs@MP powder with UV light of 365 nm on and off; (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CDs@MP; (c) Phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CDs@MP; (d) Emission color coordinates of fluorescence and phosphorescence (λex = 375 nm) of N, S-CDs@MP powder; (e) Fluorescence decay curves of N, S-CDs@MP; (f) Phosphorescence decay curves of N, S-CDs@MP; (g) Phosphorescence intensity of N, S-CDs@MP with varied temperature; (h) Normalized luminescence and phosphorescence spectra of N, S-CDs@MP powder at low temperature (77 K) under excitation of 360 nm; (i) Illustration of the possible triple-mode emission process of N, S-CDs@MP powder; (j) DSC trances of N, S-CDs@MP.

(a) Photographs of N, S-CDs@MP in pH = 1–13 solvents excited by 365 nm UV lamp and after switching off UV; (b) Photographs of N, S-CDs@MP in various polar solvents under excitation of 365 nm UV lamp, and after switching off UV.
(a) Images of the word 'Datong' under daylight, with UV ON and UV OFF. (b) Images of pattern “8888” under daylight, with UV ON and UV OFF. (c) Images of flower under daylight, with UV ON and UV OFF.
(a) Synthetic route of the N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs@MP composites. (b) Schematic illustration of the generation of RTP (EX: Excite, FL: Fluorescence, Vib.: Vibration).

head.ce:title: Ultra-stable room temperature phosphorescence CDs-based composite materials for anti-counterfeiting application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Counterfeit and shoddy products exist widely in our daily life. Effective anti-counterfeiting means have become an issue with increasing concern. Herein, we proposed a new strategy for dispersing N, S-CDs into melamine phosphate (MP) to prepare N, S-CDs@MP composite. The co-doping of carbon dots with heteroatoms (N and S atoms) in conjunction with matrix protection synergistically enhanced the emission of long-lived room temperature phosphorescence (RTP). The obtained N, S-CDs@MP composite exhibited green RTP with phosphorescence quantum yield of 9.27 % and lifetime of 489 ms. More importantly, even in extreme conditions, the RTP of N, S-CDs@MP in the dispersion still persisted. The excellent RTP properties and good stability made N, S-CDs@MP a promising material for advanced anti-counterfeiting applications. This study afforded a new RTP material which could be easily prepared and used for information encryption and graphic anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of science and technology, the problem of counterfeiting and shoddy products is becoming more and more serious, so anti-counterfeiting technology has attracted considerable attention. Thus, many efforts have been made towards developing advanced strategies to protect valuable items from being replicated [1]. Among various anti-counterfeiting strategies, the implementation of anti-counterfeiting labels has gained significant attention due to their high throughput, ease of application, and design flexibility. The unique properties of RTP materials with high availability and phosphorescent typical durations make them easy to use as a new anti-counterfeiting technology [2]. However, the synthesis of RTP materials with excellent properties is always a challenge, which attribute to the spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions. Current RTP materials mainly include inorganic materials containing rare earth elements [3], precious metal composite [4–6] and pure organic compounds [7,8]. Nevertheless, these materials are often limited by inherent drawbacks such as high-cost, relatively complicated preparation procedure, unstable luminescence properties and potential biological toxicity [9]. Thus, developing novel RTP materials with facile preparation, low cost, tiny toxicity and stable optical behavior are highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging class of luminescent nanomaterials, have attracted much interest owing to their low cytotoxicity, good biostability, easy synthesis and stable optical properties [10]. Up to now, various fluorescent CDs were developed and widely applied in biological imaging [11,12], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [13,14], photocatalysis [15,16] and sensors [17–19]. While, CDs with phosphorescence property were rarely reported due to the spin forbidden transition feature of triplet-excited states and the nonradiative deactivation. Recently, unique RTP phenomena based on CDs has been discovered. Zhao and coauthors [20] reported a CDs@polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite, the corresponding further obtained films showed a green afterglow under irradiation of a 365 nm UV light. Benefiting from the rigid environment and the excellent oxygen barrier property of PVA, this CDs@PVA composite showed a long lifetime of 380 ms. Since then, several strategies have been effectively implemented to generate or enhance the phosphorescence in CDs-based materials. The introduction of heteroatoms in CDs, such as F [21], N [22,23], P [24], S [25] and B [26], can facilitate spin–orbit coupling for effectively promoting the intersystem crossing (ISC) process. Furthermore, matrix encapsulation approaches utilizing polymers [27], melamine [28], urea/biuret [22], silica [29–31], boric acid [32], zeolites [33], layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [34], and KAl(SO4)2·x(H2O) [35] have been developed to stabilize triplet excited states through hydrogen bonding, covalent interactions, rigid environments, and confinement effects. Despite these advancements, maintaining phosphorescent emission in complex environments remains a significant challenge, highlighting the need for developing robust phosphorescent CDs with environmental stability.

Herein, a novel CDs-based phosphorescence composite (N, S-CDs@MP) was successfully prepared. Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method by using folic acid (FA) and thiourea as the precursors for the first time. By connecting with melamine phosphate (MP), the composite of N, S-CDs@MP formed and displayed both blue fluorescence and green RTP. Moreover, the as-fabricated N, S-CDs@MP exhibited high stability, which could resist the quenching from strong acids and bases, water, as well as polar solvents. Considering the features of excellent RTP performances, N, S-CDs@MP could be applied in information encryption and graphic anti-counterfeiting.

FA, thiourea, hydrochloric acid (HCl), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethyl alcohol (EtOH), methyl cyanide (MeCN) and paraformaldehyde (PA) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. MP was acquired from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The ultrapure water used in all experiments was purified by the Milli-Q Plus system.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired by a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were determined with a Rigaku SmartLab SE diffractometer. FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out on Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS20 spectrometer. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded by PerkinElmer Lambda35 spectrometer. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was carried out in nitrogen atmosphere on TA Q20 differential scanning clorimeter at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Photoluminescence spectra were achieved on a PerkinElmer LS-55 photoluminescence spectrophotometer. Decay curves and absolute quantum yield were measured with a HORIBA QM 8000 spectrofluorometer. Photoluminescence spectra at 77 K were measured using an Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrofluorometer.

Typically, 0.44 g FA and 0.76 g thiourea were dissolved in 20 mL water. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and followed by heating at 200℃ for 3h. After cooling to room temperature, the crude product was obtained. Then, the crude product was centrifuged (10 min, 10,000 r/min) and filtered through a microfiltration membrane of 0.22 μm to remove the large or agglomerated particles. Finally, the filtrate was acquired by dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) for 1 day, and the powder of N, S-CDs was obtained through lyophilization.

For obtaining N, S-CDs@MP, the N, S-CDs solution (10 mg/mL, 1 mL) was first added to 10 mL of water, and 1 g MP was introduced to the N, S-CDs solution with constant stirring. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated to 200 °C for 3 h by hydrothermal treatment. Upon cooling down to room temperature, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove excess N, S-CDs. Then, the pale-yellow solids remaining at the bottom of the centrifuge tube were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 40 °C for 6 h. Eventually, the pale-yellow powder of N, S-CDs@MP was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The overall process of synthesizing N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs@MP by hydrothermal treatment was illustrated in Scheme 1 a. FA was chosen as the carbon source and nitrogen source, and thiourea served as the nitrogen source and sulfur source.

To identify the as-prepared N, S-CDs, their fluorescence spectra were initially obtained. The solution of N, S-CDs displayed a blue fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1 a), but no phosphorescence emission. Under different excitation wavelengths, the solution of N, S-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 1b). UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, S-CDs showed three typical characteristic absorption peaks at 230 nm, 270 nm and 324 nm, which belonged to the π-π* transition of C=C and n-π* transitions of C=S/C=N respectively (Fig. 1c) [36].

Subsequently, the morphology of the N, S-CDs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1d–e, TEM result revealed that N, S-CDs were dispersed as spherical particles with the diameters ranging from 1.9 nm to 3.2 nm (average for 2.2 nm). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) showed that N, S-CDs had the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which attributed to the 100 facet of graphite (Fig. 1f). The XRD patterns of N, S-CDs exhibited a strong and broad characteristic peak at approximately 26.9°, which was attributed to the (100) crystalline plane of graphitic carbon, in good agreement with HRTEM results (Fig. 1g) [37].

The FT-IR and XPS spectra were carried out to determine the surface groups and chemical compositions of N, S-CDs. To be specific, there appeared a broad stretching vibration band of O–H/N–H at 3100 ∼ 3600 cm−1, C=N at 1662 cm−1, C=C at 1599 and 1501 cm−1, C=S at 1000 ∼ 1200 cm−1 for N, S-CDs [25] (Fig. 1h). The FT-IR spectrum results exhibited that the N, S-CDs contained many functional groups that contributed to the appearance of phosphorescence, such as C=N and C=S bonds. During the formation of N, S-CDs, crosslink polymerization and dehydration reactions, the C=N and C=S were generated and acted as luminescent centers. The full XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs showed five typical peaks at 284.52 eV, 399.20 eV, 531.98 eV, 231.08 eV and 168.08 eV, indicating that N, S-CDs mainly consisted of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, S 2s, S 2p. The relative contents of C, N, O and S for N, S-CDs were calculated to be 59.68 %, 15.88 %, 22.40 % and 2.04 % (Fig. 1i and Table s1). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. s1a) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.80, 285.90 and 288.20 eV, which originated from C–C, C–N/C–O and C=N/C=O, respectively [38,39]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s band demonstrated three peaks in 398.60, 399.70 and 400.80 eV, which were mainly in the form of C–N, N–H and N=C–N respectively (Fig. s1b) [40]. The O 1s band displayed the O atom mostly in the form of two peaks at 531.30 and 532.80 eV, signifying the presence of C=O and C–O/S–O, respectively (Fig. s1c) [41,42]. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p band demonstrated two peaks in 163.90 and 168.20 eV, which were mainly in the form of C–S and C=S respectively (Fig. s1d) [25,43]. Taken together, the XPS results were in agreement with FT-IR data.

To achieve emitting the long-lived RTP, MP were introduced as the matrix to disperse N, S-CDs. Upon incorporating N, S-CDs into MP matrix, N, S-CDs@MP was explored by TEM, FT-IR spectra, XPS and XRD. TEM image of the composite also showed a spherical morphology with the average diameters of 3.0 nm, suggesting the particle size increased slightly (Fig. 2 a–b). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of N, S-CDs@MP in Fig. s2 demonstrated spherical morphology of composite. And the corresponding elemental (C, N, P, S and O) mapping images proved the successful doping of heteroatom. XRD profile of N, S-CDs@MP showed a broad characteristic peak of N, S-CDs and multiple feature peaks of MP [44] (Fig. 2c). The FT-IR spectra of MP and N, S-CDs@MP were displayed in Fig. 2d. Upon incorporation of N, S-CDs into the MP matrix, the broad absorption peak centered at 3320 cm−1 and 3070 cm−1 indicated the more prominent existence of O–H and N–H. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs@MP demonstrated a pronounced redshift, which may be ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bonds [45]. Simultaneously, XPS spectra also illustrated the functional groups of N, S-CDs@MP. The N, S-CDs@MP was primarily composed of C (36.96 %), N (40.93 %), O (18.42 %), P (2.99 %) and S (0.70 %) elements, with peaks at 288.10 eV, 531.10 eV, 399.10 eV, 190.60 eV and 133.00 eV attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, P 2p, and S 2p, respectively (Fig. 2e and Table s1). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum indicated that the peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.80 eV and 288.2 eV corresponded to C=C/C=N, C–O/C–N/C–P and C=C/C–C covalent bonds, respectively (Fig. s3a) [37]. The peaks at 398.10 eV, 399.20 eV and 400.90 eV in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum were attributed to N–H, N–P and C=N/C–N bonds respectively (Fig. s3b) [46]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum showed that the peak at 530.40 eV corresponded to C=O bonds, and the peak at 531.7 eV was responsible for P=O covalent bonds (Fig. s3c) [42]. The peaks at 132.9 eV and 133.6 eV in the high-resolution P 2p spectrum attributed to P=O and P=N bonds respectively (Fig. s3d) [46]. In the high-resolution S 2p spectrum shown in Fig. s3e, the peaks at 162.0 eV, 163.1 eV and 168.1 eV correspond to C=S, S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 bonds, respectively [25,43,47]. These findings confirmed that MP were coated in N, S-CDs successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and RTP mechanism of N, S-CDs@MP
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, photophysical properties of N, S-CDs@MP were fully investigated. As shown in Fig. s4, the absorption peaks of N, S-CDs@MP at 240 nm, 280 nm and 355 nm might be caused by the π-π* transition of C=C and n-π* transitions of C=S/C=N, respectively, in agreement with the behavior of N, S-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the resultant N, S-CDs@MP powder exhibited blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Especially, a longer duration of green RTP (lasting for nearly 8 s to naked eye) could be observed at ambient conditions after ceasing UV irradiation. Specifically, the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission peaks appeared at 370 nm and 445 nm (Fig. 3b), and the maximum phosphorescence excitation and emission peaks were obtained at 380 nm and 560 nm, respectively (Fig. 3c). From the phosphorescence emission spectrum, an obvious wavelength redshift compared to fluorescence emission spectrum were observed, implying the afterglow nature of phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence. As displayed in Fig. 3d, the phosphorescence chromaticity coordinates of N, S-CDs@MP was (0.405, 0.486), close to the green color, and the fluorescence chromaticity coordinates was (0.155, 0.117), close to the blue region, which were consistent with the emission spectra. Moreover, N, S-CDs@MP showed an average fluorescence lifetime of 5.64 ns (Fig. 3e, Table s2). To gain more insights into the properties of N, S-CDs@MP, the phosphorescence decay trace was further measured and plotted (Fig. 3f). The decay spectrum was fitted by three components of lifetime as 23.46 ms (1.81 %), 97.87 ms (6.25 %) and 495.04 ms (91.93 %), which indicated the system possessed multiple decay channels, implying the existence of different electronic transition processes [44]. Especially, the average lifetime of N, S-CDs@MP was calculated to be 489 ms, indicating long-lived RTP property (Table s3). The absolute RTP quantum yield was measured to be 7.4 % at ambient conditions under the excitation of 380 nm.

To address whether the afterglow of N, S-CDs@MP were phosphorescence or delayed fluorescence, the phosphorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs@MP under different temperatures were obtained [48]. Fig. 3g showed that the emission intensity of RTP decreased with increasing temperature from 77 K to 298 K, which probably due to the effective restriction of the molecule vibration and nonradiative transition of triplet excitons at low temperatures. Therefore, these results further confirmed that the emission of N, S-CDs@MP was indeed RTP emission and not thermally activated delayed fluorescence emission.

Furthermore, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of N, S-CDs@MP at 77 K were measured to estimate the energy gap (ΔEST) between the lowest singlet excited state (S1) and the lowest triplet excited state (T1) in the ISC process. As revealed in Fig. 3h, the low-temperature fluorescence spectrum of N, S-CDs@MP exhibited a peak at 450 nm, and the corresponding phosphorescence spectrum showed a peak at 560 nm. ΔEST of N, S-CDs@MP was calculated to be 0.54 eV (S1 = 2.75 eV and T1 = 2.21 eV, respectively), demonstrating a relatively narrow energy gap between S1 and T1 (Table s4). Such a small magnitude of ΔEST benefited in effective ISC processes to populate triplet excitons [37] (Fig. 3i).

Based on the above discussion, the possible mechanism for realizing RTP was proposed. On the one hand, N and S atoms could be integrated into carbon cores to form amorphous shells because similar atomic radii. The introduction of heteroatoms could form high electron density and result in molecular orbital hybridization, thus prompting the production of ISC. On the other hand, N, S-CDs@MP exhibited excellent RTP property, while N, S-CDs could only emit fluorescence, indicating that the MP matrix played a key role in the generation of phosphorescence. Served as a protecting matrix, the MP could lock and separate luminescent centers in N, S-CDs, which effectively stabilized the triplet state by restraining nonradiative transition. Besides, the hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of N, S-CDs interacted with MP and formed hydrogen bonds. To clarify the hydrogen bonds in detail, DSC was also applied for N, S-CDs@MP (Fig. 3j). More precisely, N, S-CDs@MP exhibited a broad endothermic transition during the heating phase owing to the destroyed hydrogen bonds between N, S-CDs and MP. The formed hydrogen bonds facilitated the stability of the triplet states, thus enhancing their phosphorescence of N, S-CDs@MP. In a word, the reasons for RTP emission can be attributed to the following two points: (i) the N and S elements of N, S-CDs exhibit strong spin–orbit coupling, which can promote ISC; (ii) the triplet state of photo-excited particles can be stabilized through the role of the matrix, which promotes the generation of phosphorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Superb phosphorescence stability of N, S-CDs@MP
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the phosphorescence stability of N, S-CDs@MP, after dispersion into different solvents, neglected dissolution could be observed. The poor solubility indicated the good stability of the composite, which showed great potential for applications in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and encryption. When N, S-CDs@MP were placed in dispersion with different pH, the luminescence intensity was almost unchanged (Fig. 4 a). To further investigate the extraordinary stability of N, S-CDs@MP, they were also dispersed in different polar solvents. Similarly, N, S-CDs@MP still could show stable luminescence performance, such as in H2O, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, MeCN and PA (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the RTP color of N, S-CDs@MP in different dispersion were the same as those of the N, S-CDs@MP powder. Therefore, we speculated that the hydrogen bond skeleton formed between N, S-CDs and MP was not damaged by the water and could still provide stability to the excited triplet excitons. To sum up, the N, S-CDs@MP exhibited excellent tolerance against water, acid, base and polar solvents, which was helpful for applications such as emergency signs, photoelectric devices and anti-counterfeiting.

Inspired by the superior feature of obtained RTP materials, their potential application in information identification and data encryption were demonstrated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the pattern of word 'Datong' could emit bright green phosphorescence. The pattern “8888” and the logo of a flower were written on nonfluorescent filter paper using N, S-CDs@MP composite and interference CDs (Fig. 5b–c). Upon excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp, wrong information (the number “8888” with blue fluorescence) could be readily observed. However, after ceasing the UV lamp, the pattern turned green and the encryption feature “2024” appeared under phosphorescent mode. Similarly, the flower pattern emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. When switching off the excitation, only the petals were captured by naked eyes. The petals parts exhibited green phosphorescence, but the leaves were invisible. Apparently, these results demonstrated that the N, S-CDs@MP composite could be potentially applied in high-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, we have successfully developed a novel composite with ultra-stable RTP properties by embedding N, S-CDs within a MP matrix. The efficient RTP of N, S-CDs@MP was achieved by N, S doping and the assistance of MP matrix. N, S-CDs@MP composite displayed long lifetime up to 489 ms, and the green phosphorescence could be observed for even 8 s with the naked eyes. Besides, N, S-CDs@MP composite exhibited outstanding stability in the case of acids, bases and polar solvents, because MP matrix possessed outstanding physic-chemical stability. MP could effectively stabilize the triplet state by suppressing the nonradiative processes and hindering the oxygen and moisture quenching. Finally, N, S-CDs@MP was applied to advanced message encryption for text and images, demonstrating its great potential in the realm of information security. Thus, the prepared RTP material has enormous development potential in anti-counterfeiting pattern and information encryption.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22476118), Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2022L424, 2023L271), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202303021211324, 202303021222212), Fund Program for the Scientific Activities of Selected Returned Overseas Professionals in Shanxi Province (20230036), Fundamental Research Program of Datong City (2023065), Key Scientific and Technological Projects of Datong City (2024054), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Datong University (2022K18) and Postgraduate science and technology innovation project of Shanxi Datong University (23CX24).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126106.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
